segments.s462-g105: چیدمان: an independent clause meaning “we have not seen anyone like him go to Rome.” ندیدیم is the past perfective ‘we did not see’ (negated past), ماننده ‘like/similar to’ directly modifies او, and به روم uses به + place (here ‘toward/to Rome’). The implied subject of آمدست in the next segment is the same ‘he’ (او).
segments.s462-g106: دلیر آمدست is an archaic/poetic perfective construction: آمدست = ‘has come’ (older form of آمده است) with the adjective دلیر before it (‘brave has come’). اندرین is a fused prepositional determiner ‘in this/into this’, and مرز و بوم is a coordinated noun phrase ‘border and homeland’. Together this gives ‘(he) the brave has come into this border and land’; the subject (he) is carried over from the previous clause.
segments.s462-g107: همی is an adverb meaning ‘still/continuously/ever’; برگراید (from گراییدن) is a 3rd-person verb ‘turns/inclines (toward)’; the phrase سپاه ترا contains سپاه ‘army’ and the enclitic ت (2nd‑person) + را, here marking the addressee/possessive/definite object ‘you/your’. Word order is adverb + verb + noun phrase.
segments.s462-g108: همان introduces the noun list as ‘that/the very’; the three nouns گنج و تخت و کلاه are coordinated items (‘treasure and throne and hat/helmet/crown’). The enclitic ت attached to the final noun (کلاه ت) plus the following را makes the whole list definite/possessive: ‘your treasure, throne, and crown’ (classical Persian often attaches possessive to the last noun to govern the whole series).
segments.s462-g109: چو is the temporal conjunction ‘when’. گفت فرستاده = ‘the messenger said’ (گفت is past tense). بشنید شاه is a poetic word order where the past verb بشنید ‘(he) heard’ appears before its subject شاه; taken together: ‘When the messenger spoke, the king heard.’
segments.s462-g110: فزون کرد نگاه is a light-verb idiom: فزون ‘more/more intensely’ + کرد ‘made/did’ + نگاه ‘gaze’ = ‘made/heightened his gaze’ (i.e. stared more intently). سوی سکندر is a prepositional phrase ‘toward Alexander’ (سوی = toward). Word order here: adverb/qualifier + light verb + directional phrase + noun object.
segments.s462-g111: سکندر is the subject. بدانست is a compound past meaning ‘he knew/realized’ (ب + دانست = ‘knew it’). کاندر is an archaic fused conjunctional phrase (که + اندر) meaning ‘that in/wherein’ and introduces the subordinate phrase; نهان is an adverb/adjective ‘hidden/secretly’. Together: ‘Alexander knew that in secret…’
segments.s462-g112: چه introduces an indirect question/object clause ‘what’, گفتند is past plural ‘they said’, and با شهریار جهان is a prepositional phrase meaning ‘to/with the king of the world’ (با here functions like ‘to’ in classical usage with گفتن). The whole clause = ‘what they had said to the king of the world.’
segments.s462-g65: پیامم (پیام + ـم) is a direct-object/possessive form meaning “my message” or here simply “the message to me”; سپهبد is the agent “the spahbed” placed after the object (classical Persian often puts object before subject). بدین گونه is a frozen prepositional phrase meaning “in this way / thus” (بدین = به + این, ‘in this’; گونه = ‘manner’). داد is the past simple verb “gave.” Together this clause literally reads “my-message the spahbed in-this-manner gave.”
segments.s462-g66: بگفتم is the 1st‑person past “I said/told.” به شاه marks the indirect object “to the king.” آنچ (آن‌چ) is a relative/indefinite pronoun “that which / what.” او کرد is a perfective clause “he had done” (او = he; کرد = did/made). یاد here functions as a noun meaning “reminder/recall” and ties to بگفتم: “I said to the king (the) that-which he had done (as) a reminder.” Word order follows classical Persian: verb-final within the relative clause, main verb (بگفتم) fronted.
segments.s462-g67: بیاراستندش is a compound past verb (ب + آراستن with object pronoun ـش) meaning “they prepared/they arrayed him/it”; the object clitic ـش attaches to the verb. یکی جایگاه: یکی here has the indefinite sense “a certain / a” and جایگاه is the noun “place/seat/position.” The clause order is verb (with clitic) then noun phrase (typical of the epic style where the verb can precede or follow).
segments.s462-g68: چنانچون is an adverbial conjunction “just as / such as,” introducing a simile or manner. بود is the past tense copula “was.” درخور modifies پایگاه and means “worthy of / appropriate to”; پایگاه here denotes “rank/place/station.” The sequence gives a predicate: “it was in the manner appropriate to his station.”
segments.s462-g69: سپهدار ایران is a nominative NP “the commander of Iran” (title + genitive place). چو is a temporal conjunction here meaning “when.” بنهاد is a past tense verb “placed/laid/established” (here ‘set’ the feast/table). خوان is the object meaning “feast/table/banquet.” The clause reads “when the commander of Iran set the banquet…”
segments.s462-g70: به سالار فرمود is “he said/commanded to the chief” (به marks the indirect object; فرمود is a polite/epic past form ‘he ordered/said’). کو را بخوان is an interrogative/imperative phrase—کو here is an old interrogative meaning roughly “who/whom” or “where/which” in classical usage, را is the object marker, and بخوان is the present/immediate imperative/infinitive from خواندن used as “call/summon/recite.” Literally: “He said to the chief, ‘Whom call (you)?’”
segments.s462-g71: چو is the temporal subordinating conjunction “when.” نان خورده شد is a perfect construction: نان (bread) + خورده (past participle ‘eaten’) + شد (auxiliary ‘became/was’), giving “when the bread had been eaten.” مجلس آراستند follows with مجلس as the subject/object ‘the assembly/banquet’ and آراستند (past plural) “they set/arranged/adorned,” so together: “When the bread was eaten, they arranged the assembly.”
segments.s462-g72: This is a coordination: می‌و رود و رامشگران خواستند. The verbs are plural past خواستند “they wanted/asked for.” The list before it names what they requested: می‌و (here written joined but contextually = می و, ‘wine and’) and رود (here the musical instrument, a stringed lute) joined by و, then رامشگران “the entertainers/comforters” as another coordinated item. The overall meaning: “they desired wine, (the) lute, and entertainers.” Note: some lemmas in the provided analysis are nonstandard/contracted (classical word joins), but structurally this is a simple coordinated object list followed by the plural past verb.
segments.s462-g81: بدو (preverbal) is a dative/locative prepositional phrase meaning “to him/unto him,” placed before the verb for emphasis; گفت is the past tense verb “said” (here used with a person speaking) — the predicate is thus “said to him.” ساقی is the agent “the cupbearer.” که‌ای + شیر فش is a vocative phrase: که‌ای is an older/exclamatory vocative marker (equivalent to “O”), and شیر فش is an epithet addressed to the addressee (“lion-…”, i.e. a heroic/forcible epithet). The whole segment is “The cupbearer said to him, ‘O lion-fist…’.”
segments.s462-g82: چه داری is a present interrogative clause: چه “what” + داری (present tense of داشتن) “do you have / are you holding.” همی is an archaic/dialectal preverbal particle here marking a continuing or immediate action (roughly “therein / in that very …” / continuous). جام زرین is the noun phrase object “the golden cup.” به کش is an imperative: کش (verb “pull, draw”) in the imperative “draw!” and به here combines with کش to give the sense “draw forth / pull out” (a separable, idiomatic compound). The whole clause asks and immediately commands: “What do you have in the golden cup? Draw it forth.”
segments.s462-g83: سکندر is the subject “Alexander.” چنین داد پاسخ is a stock expression: چنین “thus/so” + داد (past of دادن) “gave” + پاسخ “an answer” — together meaning “so answered” or “answered thus.” که introduces the reported content clause (“that …”). جام begins that clause as its topic/subject: “that the cup …”.
segments.s462-g84: فرستاده را: فرستاده “the envoy/messenger” with را after it marks its definite/associative role in the clause (here indicating possession/association with the cup: “the envoy’s”). باشد is the copula in the subjunctive/hortative register (classical/narrative usage) functioning as “is” / “belongs to.” ای نیک‌نام is a vocative: ای “O” + نیک‌نام “well‑named / reputable”; Alexander addresses the addressee respectfully. The clause thus states the cup’s ownership: “the cup is the envoy’s, O honorable one.”
segments.s462-g85: گر is the conditional particle “if.” آیین ایران is the noun phrase “the custom/practice of Iran” (آیین = custom/rite; ایران = Iran). جز اینست is the negative/exceptive clause “is other than this” or “is not this” (جز = apart from/other than; اینست = “this is”); syntactically the condition reads “If Iran’s custom is other than this ….” راه here is an interjected adverbial/marker meaning roughly “then, go (on your way)” or a rhythmic connector leading into the imperative that follows — a conventional classical placement before the imperative verb.
segments.s462-g86: ببر is the imperative “take!” (the verb بَرْدن used here as an order). جام زرین is the direct object “the golden cup” (جام = cup; زرین = golden). سوی is the preposition “toward(s).” گنج شاه is a possessive noun phrase “the king’s treasure / the royal treasury” (noun + following possessor); in Persian juxtaposition the possessor follows. Together: “Take the golden cup toward the king’s treasury.”
segments.s462-g87: بخندید is a past-tense verb “laughed”; with از آیین او it takes the sense “laughed at” (بخندید + از = to laugh at/deride). از introduces the target/reason (“at / because of the custom”), آیین او = “his/that custom.” شهریار is the subject or agent “the sovereign/king.” The sequence literally reads “The sovereign laughed at that custom.”
segments.s462-g88: یکی is an indefinite numeral/adjective “one / a certain.” جام is “cup.” پرگوهر is a compound adjective “full of gems / jewel‑studded.” شاهوار is an adjective meaning “kingly / regal.” This noun phrase, with its articlelike یکی and stacked adjectives, names a single magnificent cup: “one jewel‑filled, kingly cup.”
segments.s462-g145: همه جنگ را is a fronted, definite object phrase (‘the whole battle’), with را marking it as object. تیغها is the grammatical subject (swords), and برکشید is a past-tense, 3rd-person plural verb meaning “drew up/drew (their swords).” The clause literally reads “(as for) the whole battle, the swords drew,” i.e. “they drew their swords for the whole battle.”
segments.s462-g146: وزین دشت هامون functions as a locative/nominal phrase meaning ‘from this/plain of Hamun’ (in classical usage وزین is a fused form conveying ‘و زِ این’ or an epithet for the plain). سر is the noun ‘head,’ and اندرکشید is a past 3rd-plural verb meaning ‘drew up / lifted’ (here ‘lifted up their heads’). The verb agrees with the understood plural agents from the previous clause.
segments.s462-g147: چو introduces a temporal subordinate clause (‘when’). در جنگ is a locative phrase ‘in battle,’ تن را is the definite object ‘the body’ (را marking it), and به رنج is a prepositional phrase ‘to/into hardship.’ آورید is a past 3rd-person plural verb meaning ‘they brought/caused’ (here: ‘they subjected [their bodies] to hardship’).
segments.s462-g148: ازان رنج is ‘from that suffering’ (ازان = از آن, ‘from that’). شاهی و گنج are the coordinated direct objects ‘kingship/kingdom and treasure.’ آورید repeats the past 3rd-plural verb and here means ‘they obtained/brought (to themselves).’ The clause expresses result: by suffering they gained kingship and treasure.
segments.s462-g149: جهان آفریننده is a compound noun ‘world‑creator’ (i.e., the Creator/God). یار is the predicate noun ‘companion/ally,’ and منست is an archaic copular expression meaning ‘is mine / is with me’ (classical contraction of a copula). So the clause states that the Creator is the speaker’s ally.
segments.s462-g150: سر اختر is a nominal phrase in which سر means ‘chief/leader’ (chief among the stars). اندر کنار is a locative expression ‘at/in the side (beside),’ and منست again functions as the archaic copula ‘is with me / belongs to me.’ The clause therefore says the foremost star stands at the speaker’s side.
segments.s462-g151: بزرگان is the plural subject ‘the great ones/elders.’ برو here is an archaic or adverbial particle (roughly ‘thereupon’ or a linking exclamation) rather than the literal verb ‘go’; خواندند is past 3rd-person plural ‘they sang/recited/called out,’ and آفرین is the acclamation ‘bravo’ or ‘praise!’ So the elders cried out an acclamation.
segments.s462-g152: که introduces the content of the acclamation (‘that’ / ‘saying that’). آباد بادا is an optative formula: آباد = ‘prosperous,’ بادا = ‘may it be,’ so together ‘may (it) be prosperous.’ به قیصر زمین is a dative/benefactive phrase ‘to the Caesar of the land’ (i.e., for the emperor). The whole line is an oath/blessing: ‘May the land be prosperous for the Caesar.’
segments.s462-g137: بدیدند is a past-tense, third-person plural verb meaning "they saw/found." شب here functions adverbially as "that night." شاه را uses the object marker را to mark شاه as the definite direct object of بدیدند. شادکام is a predicate adjective meaning "joyful/fortunate" describing the king; the clause therefore reads literally “they saw the king (شاه را) joyful.”
segments.s462-g138: This verbless clause gives the scene in front of the palace: به پیش = “before/in front of” (به + پیش), اندرون is the inner apartments/inner quarters, and پرگهر is a compound adjective meaning “jeweled/precious.” چار جام is the noun phrase “four cups.” Word order is prepositional phrase + location + adjectival modifier + numeral + noun.
segments.s462-g139: به گردان = “to the troop” (به as the preposition “to/for,” گردان = “troop/unit”). چنین introduces reported speech: “thus.” گفت would be the verb “said/spoke,” but the provided senses do not list that verbal sense (see senses). کاباد is a proper name (or place-name) here, and بید in this position is an archaic/poetic copula parallel to بود ("was"); the listed senses do not supply that meaning, so the historical copula function should be noted.
segments.s462-g140: بدین (here split as ب + دین) functions adverbially as “by/with this” or “thus.” Because the segmentation split the unit, ب is the prepositional/prefixal element “by/with” while دین as split does not correspond to the intended demonstrative and so has no matching sense in the provided list. فرخی modifies فال and means “auspicious/fortunate” (not necessarily the proper name Farrokhi here). فال ما شاد بید is reported-predication: فالِ ما "our omen/fortune" + شاد "happy/auspicious" + بید (archaic copula = "was").
segments.s462-g141: که introduces an explanatory clause "that/for" here. این جام = “this cup.” پیروزی is a noun "victory" and جانِ ما "our life/soul" is in the genitive relation; the clause ends with ست, the poetic/archaic reduced form of است (the copula) meaning “is.” The literal structure: “that this cup is the victory of our life.”
segments.s462-g142: سر اختران is a nominal phrase—سر “head/top” followed by اختران (here meaning “the stars,” although the provided senses for اختران do not include that meaning). زیر فرمان ماست is a predicate: زیر فرمان = “under the command/control of,” ماst = “is (ours).” The clause therefore states that even the stars’ heads are subject to our command. Note that اختران’s correct lexical sense (“stars”) is not present in the provided sense list, so that item is marked accordingly.
segments.s462-g143: هم از لشکرش برگرفتم شمار is a compact statement: هم "also/too," از لشکرش "from/of his army," برگرفتم is a first-person past verb meaning “I took up/removed/collected” but here best rendered “I counted/numbered,” and شمار = “number/count.” Word order is topic/adverb + prepositional phrase + past verb + noun object.
segments.s462-g144: فراوان کم است از شنیده سوار is a comparison: فراوان "much/many" (subject), کم است "is small/few," از introduces the comparison "compared to/with," شنیده here means “renowned/famed” (participle/adjective), and سوار = “rider/horseman.” The sense: their abundant numbers are nevertheless small when compared with the famed horsemen (the cavalry) they had heard about.
segments.s462-g25: همانگه چو ... is a temporal clause: همانگه (an archaic/poetic adverb) + چو = 'as/just when'. بنشست is the simple past (3rd sing.) 'he sat down'. بر پای خاست is a light-verb phrase meaning 'rose to his feet' (بر + پای + خاست, خاست = 'arose'). The subject is omitted (3rd person) and understood from context.
segments.s462-g26: پیام سکندر is the object phrase 'Alexander's message' (possessive word order: message + possessor). بیاراست is a past tense (3rd sg.) poetic form of 'to arrange/compose/adorn' and here functions as the main verb 'he composed/arranged'. راست is an adverbial/qualifying word 'straight/straightforward' (intensifying how the message was prepared). The sentence omits an explicit subject, continuing the 3rd‑person narration.
segments.s462-g27: نخست is an adverb 'first/firstly'. آفرین here is a noun/exclamation 'praise/commendation' and with کرد (past 'did') forms the light-verb construction آفرین کرد = 'uttered praise / praised'. بر شهریار is the target of the praise 'to the king' (بر + direct addressee).
segments.s462-g28: که introduces a following wish/clause; جاوید + بادا is a standard optative formula ('جاوید بادا' = 'may [he] be eternal'), with بادا being the optative auxiliary 'may it be'. سر تاج‌دار is a nominal phrase 'the crown-bearing head' (سر = head, تاج‌دار = crowned). The whole clause is a wish for the king's everlasting rule.
segments.s462-g29: سکندر چنین گفت = 'Alexander thus said' where چنین modifies the manner of گفت. کای is a vocative particle 'O' addressing someone, and نیک‌نام is the vocative complement 'well‑reputed one'. Note: گفت is used as a speech verb here (past 3rd); the senses list given does not include the simple verbal sense 'said'.
segments.s462-g30: به گیتی = 'in/throughout the world' (به + گیتی). بهرجای functions adverbially 'in every place/everywhere'. گسترده modifies کام ('broad/widespread desire'); کام = 'desire/wish'. Together they form the wish 'may your desire be widespread through the world.'
segments.s462-g31: مرا آرزو نیست is an existential negative construction: مرا (dative/object pronoun 'for me') + آرزو (wish/desire) + نیست ('is not') = 'I have no desire'. با شاه جنگ ('with the king' + 'war') names the thing desired (i.e. 'to wage war with the king'). Word order places the experiencer (مرا) before the noun and the negative copula follows the noun, a common Persian pattern.
segments.s462-g32: نه is the coordinating negation 'nor/not'. بر بوم ایران گرفتن is an infinitival/verbal-noun sequence 'to take/seize the land of Iran' (بر + بوم ایران + گرفتن). گرفتن here means 'to take/capture'. درنگ ('delay/tarry') is a noun indicating lingering; in this context the phrase says he has no desire to seize Iran and then delay (i.e. to occupy and linger).
segments.s462-g121: همه بادپایان (literally “all the wind‑feet”) is a plural noun phrase functioning as the agent: همه (everyone/all) + بادپایان (plural ‘swift‑footed’ or ‘fleet‑footed’). برانگیختند is a 3rd‑person plural past tense verb ‘they rose/sprang up/were aroused’, so the phrase means ‘all the swift‑footed rose up’. The verb agrees in number with the plural subject.
segments.s462-g122: ز پیش جهاندار is a prepositional phrase meaning ‘from/before the sovereign’: ز (from) + پیش (in the presence of/before) + جهاندار (the ruler/sovereign). بگریختند is 3rd‑person plural past ‘they fled/ran away’. The whole clause modifies/continues the action: ‘they fled from before the sovereign.’
segments.s462-g123: چو is the temporal subordinating word ‘when’. دارا here is the personal name (Dara) used as the subject. سر و افسر او is a coordinated object phrase ‘his head and (his) helmet’ — note the older style of using او after the noun as a possessive. ندید is the past tense ‘did not see’; together: ‘when Dara did not see his head and helmet…’. The negative sense comes from context (ندید = did not see).
segments.s462-g124: به تاریکی is a directional/locative phrase ‘into/into the darkness’ (به + تاریکی). از چشم شد ناپدید is a small clause ‘became unseen/from the eye’ where شد is the past of ‘become’ and ناپدید is the predicate adjective ‘vanished/invisible’. Literally ‘he became vanished from the eye’, i.e. ‘he disappeared from sight into the darkness’. Note the light verb شد used to form the stative result.
segments.s462-g125: نگهبان فرستاد is ‘(he) sent a guard’ — object (نگهبان) precedes the verb (فرستاد); the subject is omitted and understood from context. هم در زمان means ‘also/at the same time’ or ‘even in that moment’, with هم adding ‘also/even’ and در زمان (‘in/at the moment’) specifying simultaneity.
segments.s462-g126: به نزدیکی خیمه بدگمان is a goal/locative phrase ‘toward the nearness/nearby of the tent (which was) suspicious’. به + نزدیکی gives the sense ‘toward/near’, خیمه is ‘tent’, and بدگمان is an adjective ‘suspicious’ modifying خیمه; overall ‘toward the suspicious tent nearby.’
segments.s462-g127: چو رفتند is a temporal clause ‘when they left/went’. بیداردل (an epithet ‘wakeful‑hearted’ or ‘watchful one’) is the subject of the following verb phrase; رفته بود is the pluperfect ‘had gone/was gone’ (رفته + بود), so the sentence says that while ‘they’ left, the watchful one had already departed — notice the change of number (رفتند vs. رفته بود) indicating different actors.
segments.s462-g128: نه is the negation ‘not’. بخت here means ‘fate/destiny’ and introduces the comment that it was not chance; چنان ‘such/so’ qualifies پادشا ‘king/monarch’. خفته بود is ‘was asleep’ (خفته + بود, past state). Together the clause means roughly ‘it was not fate that such a king lay asleep’ — i.e. it was not mere fortune that caused so great a king to be asleep.
segments.s462-g97: بدو گفت introduces reported speech with the indirect object first: بدو (to him) + گفت (said). کاین begins the content clause ('that this...'). مهتر is a descriptive noun 'chief' and اسکندرست is the predicate: اسکندر + است contracted as -ست (archaic/poetic third-person copula), so the clause reads 'that this chief is Alexander.'
segments.s462-g98: که introduces a relative clause 'who/that' modifying مهتر. بر تخت is a locative/prepositional phrase ('on the throne') placed before the predicate. با ... و با ... links two accompanying/attendant instruments ('with a mace and with a ...'). افسرست is افسر + است (copula contracted) and here functions as a predicate ('is (with) a helmet' / 'has a helmet' in classical diction).
segments.s462-g99: بدانگه is an adverbial 'thereupon/at that moment.' که introduces the subordinate clause 'when/that.' ما را uses the object-marker را after the pronoun to mark us as the definite object of بفرمود. بفرمود is an honorific/past verb 'he commanded/said' and شاه appears after the verb (inverted subject order common in poetry): 'that the king commanded us.'
segments.s462-g100: برفتیم is simple past first-person plural 'we went/advanced.' نزدیک او is a prepositional phrase 'near him' (نزدیک + pronoun). باژخواه follows as an appositive/epithet identifying or characterizing that person (no copula needed), 'the toll-seeker/extortioner.' The overall order is verb — locative phrase — pronoun — appositive, typical of classical verse.
segments.s462-g101: برآشفت is a past-tense verb meaning 'he became angry' (compound/poetic form). The conjunction و links the next clause. ما را bears the object marker را, marking 'us' as the object of the verb phrase that follows. The sequence ب + دان + خوار کرد is the older/poetic formation equivalent to 'بدان خوار کرد' meaning 'made (us) contemptible there' (خوار = despised, کرد = 'made/caused'). The split into ب + دان reflects orthographic/morphological segmentation in the lemma list; functionally it serves as the adverbial 'there' + 'made'.
segments.s462-g102: به گفتار here is a manner phrase 'in/with speech' where به marks the mode (sense 'in (language)'). با شاه is the co-participant 'with the king.' پیکار کرد is a light-verb construction (noun پیکار 'fight/struggle' + کرد 'did') meaning 'he fought/contended' — together 'he engaged in struggle with the king,' and because of به گفتار it implies verbal contention.
segments.s462-g103: چو is a temporal conjunction here meaning 'when.' از marks separation 'from,' and پادشاهیش is 'his kingship/rule' (پادشاه + possessive suffix -یش). بگریختم is the past first-person singular 'I fled/escaped' (ب- prefix + گریختم).
segments.s462-g104: شب تیره is an adverbial phrase 'on a dark night' (night + adjective 'dark'). اسپان (plural of اسپ) is the direct object 'the horses.' برانگیختم is the past first-person singular of برانگیختن 'to rouse/rouse up' (prefix بر- + root انگیختن) meaning 'I roused/upped the horses.' The object precedes the verb, following classical SOV tendencies in poetry.
segments.s462-g129: پس (sentence-initial) = 'then/therefore' linking this action to what came before. او is the third-person pronoun 'he'. فرستاد is a simple past transitive verb 'sent' (3rd pl./sg past here agreeing with the subject 'he' performing the sending). دارا appears immediately after the verb as the direct object; here it functions as a proper name (Dara/Darius) rather than the lexical senses listed, and سوار follows as a noun 'mounted/horseman' describing Dara or his state.
segments.s462-g130: دلیران و پرخاشجویان هزار: an appositive sequence describing the force sent — دلیران 'the brave ones' and پرخاشجویان 'the quarrelsome/aggressive ones', with هزار at the end giving the number 'a thousand'. The conjunction و links the two descriptive nouns; the numeral follows in typical Persian order.
segments.s462-g131: چو باد ... همی تاختند: چو باد = 'like the wind' (چو as simile). از پس او = 'from behind him / after him' (از + پس = 'from after'), همی is the epic/perfective-continuous particle marking ongoing or vigorous action, and تاختند is past plural 'they galloped/rushed'. Word order is simile + prepositional phrase + continuous marker + verb.
segments.s462-g132: شب تیره بد راه نشناختند: the noun phrase 'شب تیره' = 'the dark night' sets time/condition; بد راه = 'bad/rough road' qualifies the route; نشناختند is past negative 'they did not recognize/know' (i.e. could not find/recognize the way). Adjective-noun order is standard and the verb comes at the end.
segments.s462-g133: طلایه بدیدند گشتند باز: طلایه 'vanguard' is the direct object of بدیدند 'they saw'. گشتند باز is a compound verbal phrase: گشتند (past plural of گشتن) here means 'they turned' and باز is the adverb 'back/again', so together 'they turned back'. The verbs are in past tense and follow the objects.
segments.s462-g134: نبد سود جز رنج و راه دراز: نبد is an archaic negative copula equivalent to نبود 'there was not'; سود 'benefit/profit' is the subject; جز introduces the exception 'except'; رنج و راه دراز lists what remains: 'hardship and a long road'. Word order is verb–subject–exception phrase, typical of older poetic Persian.
segments.s462-g135: چو اسکندر آمد به پرده‌سرای: چو here means 'when'; اسکندر is the subject 'Alexander'; آمد is simple past 'came/arrived'; به + پرده‌سرای = 'to the pavilion/audience-hall' (پرده‌سرا is an older term for a curtained hall or stage). This is a time clause introduced by چو.
segments.s462-g136: برفتند گردان رومی ز جای: برفتند 'they went/left' (past plural), گردان 'troop/detachment' is the noun, رومی qualifies it as 'Roman', and ز جای = 'from the place/position' (ز is poetic 'from'). The verb precedes the noun phrase here for compact poetic ordering but the sense is 'the Roman troop withdrew from its post'.
segments.s462-g153: فدای تو بادا is a fixed optative formula: بادا (an archaic/poetic optative ‘may it be’) gives the whole clause the meaning ‘may I/we be sacrificed for you’. فدای تو functions as the predicative phrase ‘(be) sacrificed for you’ and تن و جان ما (‘our body and soul’) names what is devoted; ما is the possessor/agent marker following the noun (classical word order permits the possessive pronoun after the possessed noun).
segments.s462-g154: برینست is a poetic fused form (برین + است) functioning as the predicative ‘is exalted / highest’ with the copula incorporated; جاوید is a postpositive adjective ‘eternal’ modifying پیمان, and پیمان ما (‘our covenant/pledge’) is the noun phrase whose quality is being predicated. Note the classical placement of the possessive pronoun ما after the noun and the omission of any extra linking words (typical in verse).
segments.s462-g155: ز شاهان is a prepositional/partitive phrase ‘of/from the kings’ and که introduces a relative clause modifying شاهان. Inside the clause یارد is used as ‘aid/helper’ (a noun with a verbal/relational force) and, with poetic ellipsis of an explicit verb, links to بدن یار تو (‘their bodies as your ally/aid’): the nouns are juxtaposed so that the sense is ‘those kings whose bodies serve as your aid/are offered as your ally’. The possessive تو follows یار in classical order.
segments.s462-g156: به introduces a list of purposes/reasons (‘for’ / ‘for the sake of’) governed by the verb/idea in the previous clause; مردی (here an abstract noun) means ‘manliness/valour’, بالا is an abstract/adjectival noun ‘loftiness/exalted rank’, and دیدار تو is ‘the meeting/seeing of you’ with the possessive pronoun after the noun. The coordinating و links the three items governed by به.
segments.s462-g89: بفرمود is an honorific/polite past tense verb 'he commanded/ordered'. تا introduces a subordinate clause of purpose/command ('so that' / 'that'), which licenses the subjunctive. بر + کفش = 'on the shoes' (بر = on; کفش = shoes). برنهند is the 3rd-person-plural subjunctive/jussive form of نهادن with the prefix بر- ('to put on/upon'), so the clause literally: 'he ordered that they put (something) on the shoes' (subject 'they' omitted). Word order is verb-final in the subordinate clause.
segments.s462-g90: یکی modifies یاقوت and means 'one' (a single); سرخ is the adjective 'red' placed before the noun here (poetic word order). یاقوت = 'ruby' (the noun). بر سر نهند = 'place on the head': بر = on, سر = head, نهند is again the 3rd-plural subjunctive/jussive of نهادن ('they place'), continuing the commanded action from the previous تا-clause. The implied subject is the same omitted group as in the previous segment.
segments.s462-g91: هم‌اندر زمان = adverbial phrase 'at the same time / meanwhile' (هم‌اندر = likewise/at the same time; زمان = time). باژ خواهان روم is a noun phrase: باژ = 'cry/clamor' and خواهان here denotes people who desire or support (literally 'seekers' or 'supporters'), so together 'those clamoring for Rome' or 'Rome-seeking partisans/cryers'. روم names the polity (Byzantium/Rome). The phrase is a temporal + topical noun phrase introducing who is being spoken of.
segments.s462-g92: کجا is the interrogative 'where' placed at the clause front. رفته بودند is the past perfect/pluperfect construction ('had gone') formed by the verbal participle رفته plus the auxiliary بودند; here بود- functions as the auxiliary (sense: auxiliary). زان = 'from that' (poetic contraction of 'از + آن'). مرز و بوم = 'border and land/country' (مرز = border; و = and; بوم = homeland). The clause asks 'where had they gone from that border and land?'.
segments.s462-g93: ز = 'from' (poetic preposition). خانه = 'the house'. ب + دان here should be read together as بدان = 'to that' (the text splits the clitic), where the printed 'دان' in the segmentation is not the lexical verb 'to know' but part of the demonstrative clitic; the provided lemma senses do not include this demonstrative use, so that token does not match those nominal senses. بزمگاه = 'banquet-hall, place of revelry'. آمدند = 3rd-person-plural past 'they came'. The whole phrase means 'they came from the house to that banquet-hall.'
segments.s462-g94: خرامان is an adverb/adv-phrase meaning 'with a proud or stately gait' and modifies the following verbal clause. به نزدیک شاه آمدند = 'they came near to the king' (به = to/toward, نزدیک = near, شاه = king). آمدند is again 3rd-plural past. Word order places the manner adverb (خرامان) before the prepositional phrase and verb.
segments.s462-g95: فرستاده is the subject 'the envoy/messenger'. روی سکندر = 'the face of Alexander' (روی = face/surface; followed by the name سکندر as its possessor). بدید is the simple past 3rd-person singular of دیدن 'he saw'. Word order here is Subject + Object + Verb (classical Persian SOV).
segments.s462-g96: بر شاه رفت = 'he went to the king' where بر (here) functions as a directional/prepositional particle 'toward/to' and شاه is the indirect object; رفت is the past tense 'he went'. آفرین گسترید = 'he spread/poured forth praise': آفرین is the noun 'praise/acclamation' placed before the verb گسترید (past 3rd-sg of گستراندن 'to spread/extend'), so the verb takes the preceding noun as its object. The subject (he) is the same envoy mentioned earlier and is omitted as is normal in Persian.
segments.s462-g1: سکندر (subject) + چو بشنید introduces a temporal clause: چو here means 'when' and بشنید is a past-tense third-person 'he heard'. کامد is an archaic/stylistic past form 'came/arrived' placed after بشنید to report what was heard; سپاه is the object 'the army'. Word order follows classical Persian inversion (temporal 'when X heard Y').
segments.s462-g2: پذیره شدن را is a nominalized clause 'the being-received' with را marking it as definite/object; بپیمود is a past verb 'he traversed/took' and راه ('road') completes the phrase. Poetically the object phrase precedes the verb, so literally 'he took the road of being received' = 'he set out to be received.'
segments.s462-g3: میان دو لشکر is a prepositional phrase 'between the two armies'; دو فرسنگ ماند uses ماند (past 'remained/stayed') with a measure phrase preceding it—classical Persian often places quantity before the verb: 'remained two parasangs.'
segments.s462-g4: سکندر is subject; گرانمایگان را is the definite object marked by را ('the illustrious ones/the nobles'); بخواند is a past verb here meaning 'he called/summoned' (an older poetic past form). The verb follows the object in inverted poetic order.
segments.s462-g5: چو 'when' introduces the clause; سیر آمد is an idiomatic expression 'became sated/satisfied' meaning 'finished hearing' or 'was satisfied'; از گفته رهنمای literally 'from the guide's words' (از here = 'from/of' indicating source), so the whole clause means 'when he was satisfied by the guide's words.'
segments.s462-g6: چنین گفت is a formulaic 'thus he said'; کاکنون ('ka-konun') is an archaic adverb 'now/at present'; جزین نیست رای literally 'there is no counsel except this' with جزین ('except this') followed by نیست 'is not' and رای 'counsel/plan' as the noun—word order mirrors classical Persian emphasis. (گفت here is the verb 'said' used in the formulaic phrase; the supplied sense list did not include the verb sense.)
segments.s462-g7: که introduces reported speech/subordinate content 'that'; من 'I'; چون here means 'as' (in the role of); فرستاده‌ای is a deverbal noun 'a messenger' (indefinite -ای) and functions as a role description; پیش اوی = 'to his presence/before him' (اوی = him). The clause expresses the hypothetical role the speaker will take.
segments.s462-g8: شوم is a subjunctive/optative 'that I may be/go' (first-person subjunctive), and برگرایم is a first-person verb 'I will turn/restore/bring back' (here 'win over' or 'convert' someone). کم و بیش 'less and more' is an idiom meaning 'to some extent / somewhat or greatly,' and اوی (him) is the object. The sequence gives the intended result: 'if I go as messenger, I will turn him, little or much.'
segments.s462-g9: کمر خواست is a past tense clause: خواست is third‑person singular past ('he wanted/asked for') with the subject omitted (understood from context). پرگوهر and شاهوار are two adjectives attributive to کمر (jeweled, kingly); Persian poetry freely orders adjectives, so they follow the verb phrase here but grammatically modify کمر.
segments.s462-g10: این عبارت is a nominal phrase introduced by یکی ('a certain / one') followed by خسروی (kingly/royal) modifying جامه (robe/garment); زرنگار is a post‑nominal adjective meaning brocade/gold‑embroidered. The whole chunk names the second item requested: 'a kingly brocade robe.'
segments.s462-g11: ببردند is past plural ('they carried/placed'). بالای is a prepositional phrase marker meaning 'on top of/above'; زرین is an adjective 'golden' modifying the following noun. ستام is the head noun here (poetic/archaic), so the structure is 'they placed (it) on the golden X.'
segments.s462-g12: به + noun phrase marks location/direction ('into / in the'); زین 'saddle' is the locative noun, اندرون 'inside' specifies interior placement, then the sequence تیغ زرین نیام describes a sword and its golden sheath (tiġ + adjective + scabbard). Word order places the location phrase before the object put there.
segments.s462-g13: This is a past action: برگزید ('he chose/selected'). سواری ده is a numeral phrase with the noun preceding the numeral (poetic inversion) meaning 'ten riders/horsemen.' از رومیان marks the origin/group 'from the Romans.'
segments.s462-g14: که introduces a relative/clausal modifier ('who/that'). دانند appears here in the present (classical Persian usage) to characterize those chosen — 'who know/are able.' هرگونه گفت و شنید is a nominalized pair meaning 'every kind of saying and hearing' i.e. 'every manner of speech and comprehension.' The clause modifies the selected horsemen.
segments.s462-g15: This is a time‑setting clause: ز لشکر بیامد is a poetic inversion meaning 'from the army came' or more idiomatically 'the army arrived.' سپیده دمان is a compound temporal adverb ('at dawn'); the two words together fix the time of arrival.
segments.s462-g16: خود 'he himself' is a focused subject pronoun; و نامداران links 'and the famed/renowned ones.' ترجمان is the head noun 'interpreter/translator.' ابا here is archaic/uncertain in isolation (contextually it relates to the group of interpreters) and functions in close apposition; grammatically the phrase lists who arrived with the army.
segments.s462-g113: همی بود is an iterative/continuous past construction: همی (kept/continually) + بود (past 'was') = 'kept on being'. تا introduces the limit 'until' and governs the clause that follows. تیره‌تر گشت روز is a verbal phrase where گشت (past 'became/turned') takes روز ('the day') as its logical subject; in older Persian word order the verb can appear before the noun subject.
segments.s462-g114: سوی باختر is a prepositional phrase 'toward the west' (سوی + باختر). گشت here is an intransitive past 'turned/moved' and گیتی‌فروز (a poetic epithet for the sun — 'world‑illuminator') is the subject placed after the verb, a frequent poetic inversion.
segments.s462-g115: بیامد is simple past 'he came'. به + noun indicates motion into/toward: به دهلیز پرده‌سرای = 'into/to the vestibule of the curtain‑hall'; پرده‌سرای qualifies دهلیز as its locus (genitive/attributive relationship).
segments.s462-g116: This is a compact verbal clause: دلاور (the warrior/hero) is the subject; به اسپ اندر آورد پای is an older word order where the verb آورد (brought/placed) precedes its object پای ('foot'). اندر here marks location ('onto/in') with به اسپ = 'onto the steed', so the whole phrase means 'the hero put/placed his foot on the horse' (i.e. mounted).
segments.s462-g117: چنین گفت پس is 'thus/so he said, then': چنین 'thus', گفت (here the past 'said') and پس 'then/afterwards'. با سواران خویش is a prepositional phrase 'with his horsemen'; خویش is expressed as the root خوی + the possessive enclitic ش, so the possession attaches to the whole phrase.
segments.s462-g118: A nominal apposition/coordination listing the companions: بلنداختر (a proper/epithetic name 'Bolandakhṭar') و 'and' نامداران خویش 'his renowned ones'. Again خویش is the possessive ('his') bound to نامداران (famed men). The sequence functions as the complement to 'with his horsemen'.
segments.s462-g119: که begins a subordinate/explanatory clause ('that'/'for'), ما را is a dative/experiencer construction ('for us' or 'to us') with را marking the cliticized pronoun, کنون 'now'. جان is the noun 'life' functioning as the theme (often postposed in classical Persian), and به اسپ اندرست is a copular locative: به اسپ 'on the steed' + اندرست (archaic verbal/copular form 'is within/in') = 'is on the horse'.
segments.s462-g120: چو introduces a conditional ('when/if'). سستی کند is a clause meaning 'weakness occurs/causes slackening' where کند is the present/past-form verb 'does/makes' used in the protasis. باد here functions as an optative particle ('may' / 'would that') rather than the noun 'wind', so باد ماند = 'may it remain'; ماند 'remain' with به دست 'in (our) hand/power' — the apodosis expresses a wish about the consequence if slackening occurs.
segments.s462-g41: چو is a conditional 'if/when' introducing the clause. رزم آوری is a 2nd-person present/imperative-style form ('you bring/wage battle') where رزم (battle) is the object/compliment and آوری is the verb 'you bring/wage'. باتو ('with you') follows the verb, and رزم آورم is a 1st-person present/future statement ('I bring/wage battle / I will fight') where آورم is the verb agreeing with the speaker.
segments.s462-g42: ازین بوم is literally 'from this land' (از + این written here as ین) with بوم 'homeland/soil'. بی‌رزم is an adjectival compound 'without battle / un-battled'. برنگذرم is a 1st-person negative form meaning 'I will not pass/go away' (here: 'I will not leave/go away from this place without fighting').
segments.s462-g43: گزین کن is an imperative formed by the verb root (گزین) plus the auxiliary کن 'do' — 'choose!' یکی روزگار نبرد is the direct object phrase 'one time/day of battle' (یکی modifies روزگار; نبرد specifies the kind of day). The phrase requests selecting a single day for fighting.
segments.s462-g44: برین باش is an imperative phrase: برین (بر + این, 'on/at that') + باش 'be!' — 'be present on that (day)'. و is the coordinating 'and'. زین آرزو برمگرد is an imperative 'turn back/withdraw from this desire' where زین functions as 'from this' (classical contraction) and برمگرد is the verb 'turn back / return' used here as 'abandon/withdraw'.
segments.s462-g45: که introduces a subordinate/explanatory clause ('for/that'). من is the subject 'I'. سر نپیچم is a negative present/future construction: سر 'head' + نپیچم (negative of پیچیدن 'to turn/twist') used idiomatically to mean 'I will not turn away / I will not yield'. ز جنگ سران is a prepositional phrase 'from the war of the chiefs' (ز = from; جنگ = war; سران = chiefs/leaders) specifying what he will not turn away from.
segments.s462-g46: وگر is a conditional 'if / but if'. چند here means 'however many / however much' (i.e., however great a number). باشد is the present subjunctive/optative form of 'to be' used after the conditional sense (loosely 'there be' or 'may be'). سپاهی گران is a noun phrase 'heavy/weighty army(s)' where سپاهی 'soldiers/army' is qualified by گران 'heavy/formidable' (i.e., numerous or powerful forces).
segments.s462-g47: چو is a temporal conjunction 'when'. دارا here is the proper name Dārā (appearing in the text as the same form as the adjective/ noun 'possessor'); بدید is the simple past 'saw'. آن دل و رای او is a noun phrase 'that heart and counsel of his' (آن = that; دل = heart; رای = counsel/plan; او = his), indicating what Dārā perceived.
segments.s462-g48: This segment lists what followed: سخن گفتن is the verbal noun/infinitive 'to speak / speaking' or 'speech' + 'to say' (here functioning as an object: he 'spoke' or 'spoke of'). و links items; فر is the technical epic term 'farr' meaning royal glory or divine radiance; بالای او means 'his superiority / preeminence' (بالا = top/above, with genitive suffix 'ـِ او'). The whole string names the things he spoke about or pronounced praise of.
segments.s462-g33: برآنم is a fixed predicative phrase meaning “I am resolved / I intend.” که introduces a subordinate clause that follows. گرد زمین اندکی is an adverbial phrase (‘around the earth, a little’) placed before the verb; گرد here functions spatially (‘around’), زمین is the noun it modifies, and اندکی is the quantifier ‘a little’.
segments.s462-g34: بگردم and ببینم are two first-person present/optative verbs with the prefixed ب-; بگردم (‘I may/will roam’) is the main verbal action of the subordinate clause introduced by که, and ببینم (‘I may see’) follows as a coordinated or purpose-like verb (‘and see / so that I may see’). جهان را is the direct object marked by را, and یکی is an indefinite pronoun meaning ‘someone / anyone’ (i.e. ‘to see whether there is anyone in the world’).
segments.s462-g35: همه راستی خواهم places همه (‘all’) before راستی (‘truth/uprightness’) as the object(s) of خواهم; خواهم is first-person present/future of خواستن and conveys ‘I will desire / I will seek.’ The conjunction و links نیکویی (‘goodness’) as an additional coordinated object of خواهم.
segments.s462-g36: به ویژه is a fixed phrase meaning ‘especially.’ که here introduces the reason/cause (‘because / since’) and connects that reason to the preceding clause. سالار ایران تویی is a plain nominal sentence in which تویی is the poetic second-person present copula (‘thou art’), identifying the addressee as ‘the lord (سالار) of Iran.’
segments.s462-g37: اگر introduces a conditional clause. خاک داری تو literally reads ‘you have earth/ground’ (word order puts the pronoun تو after the verb for emphasis). از من دریغ is a short expression meaning ‘[do not] withhold from me’ or ‘do not be sparing toward me’; the full negative sense is completed by the following clause (نشاید).
segments.s462-g38: نشاید is an impersonal negative predicate meaning ‘it is not fitting / it ought not to be.’ سپردن is the infinitive ‘to hand over / to entrust’; هوا را (literally ‘the air’) functions here as the object and poetically stands for breath or life. چو میغ is a simile ‘like a blade,’ modifying سپردن; together the clause says it is improper to entrust one’s breath (life) to the sword.
segments.s462-g39: چنین is an adverb meaning ‘thus/so,’ placed before the adverbial phrase با سپاه (‘with an army’) which modifies آمدی (‘you came’). پیش من (‘before me / in my presence’) is a locative phrase following the verb and object, with the pronoun من as its object.
segments.s462-g40: نه negates the following noun phrase (آگاهی). آگاهی از رای من means ‘knowledge of my counsel/plan’ where از introduces the source/relationship and رای is here ‘counsel/plan.’ کم بیش is an idiomatic pair meaning ‘little or much’ (used with the negation to indicate absence of any knowledge at all). من marks the possessor of رای (‘my counsel’).
segments.s462-g17: چو here is a temporal conjunction 'when' placed before the verb آمد; آمد is simple past 'came'. به نزدیک is a prepositional phrase 'to/near' (به + نزدیک) indicating motion toward proximity. دارا فراز is a name/epithet in postnominal order: دارا (the ruler's name/possession sense) followed by فراز 'on high/lofty', functioning like an epithet 'Dara the High.'
segments.s462-g18: پیاده شد is a resultative expression: پیاده (on foot / dismounted) + شد (past of شدن 'became'), here meaning 'he dismounted / became on foot'. و simply coordinates the two actions. برد پیشش نماز: برد is the past of بردن 'to take/bring' used transitively here with نماز as its object, and پیشش 'before him/in his presence' marks the location/beneficiary of the action — together meaning 'he performed/took prayer before him.'
segments.s462-g19: جهاندار is a title 'the world-ruler/sovereign' and stands as the subject. دارا follows as the name/epithet of that ruler. مر is an archaic clitic meaning 'to me / for me' (an indirect-object element) placed before the pronoun; او را is the direct object 'him' (را marks a definite object). بخواند is past tense (3rd sg) of خواندن used here in the sense 'to call/summon' (had him called).
segments.s462-g20: بپرسید is past tense (3rd pl or formal past) 'he asked / questioned' here continuing the sequence of actions. و coordinates the next clause. بر زیر گاهش is a locative phrase: بر + زیر + گاهش (گاه + possessive -ش) literally 'on/at the lower part of his place' — idiomatically 'at the foot/under the throne/place of him.' نشاند is an archaic transitive past of نشستن 'to seat / set (someone) down' meaning 'he caused him to sit / set him down.'
segments.s462-g21: همه نامداران is the plural subject 'all the famed/renowned ones.' فروماندند is a past plural verb; in this context it carries the archaic sense 'were abashed/left humbled / were put down' (not simply 'remained'), and agrees in number with the plural subject (‑ند ending).
segments.s462-g22: بروبر is an adverb meaning 'face-to-face / directly' or 'exactly' and modifies the manner. نهان is another adverb 'secretly / privately'. آفرین here is an exclamation 'bravo / praise' and خواندند is past plural 'they uttered/sang' — together 'they privately uttered “afrin” (bravo).' Note the word order: manner adverbs precede the verb خواندند.
segments.s462-g23: ز is the poetic/prepositional 'from'. دیدار is a noun 'the sight / seeing' and takes the following genitival phrase. آن modifies فر 'that (royal) glory/splendour' and فرهنگ او is 'his culture/refinement' with the possessor pronoun او following the noun. The clause lists what the following effects come from ('from the sight of that glory and his refinement').
segments.s462-g24: The prepositions repeat for emphasis: ز (from) بالا (height/loftiness) 'from his loftiness' (بالا nominalized) و links items. از again 'from' introduces a new item: شاخ (literally 'branch' or 'horn', here metaphorical) and آهنگ او ('his bearing/melody/intonation' — آهنگ can mean melodic or manner); او is the possessive pronoun. The phrase is poetic enumerative language ('from his height and from his branch and bearing').
segments.s462-g57: چنین (thus) is an adverb placed before the verb داد (gave) to modify the manner of the reply; داد here is a past-tense lexical verb meaning 'gave' and forms the main verb of the verbless clause 'داد پاسخ' = 'gave an answer'. پاسخ is the direct object 'answer/reply' following داد. که introduces a subordinate clause that reports the content of the reply; in that clause این کس نکرد is a short negative past clause where نکرد is the negative past of کردن 'to do' and کس refers to 'this person' (here pointing to a particular man).
segments.s462-g58: نه ... و نه is the correlative negation 'neither ... nor ...'. در آشتی is a prepositional phrase literally 'in reconciliation/peace' (در + آشتی), while اندر نبرد uses the archaic preposition اندر 'in/within' before نبرد 'battle' to give 'in battle/war'. Both phrases complete the negative predicate of the preceding clause (explaining in which situations the action did not occur).
segments.s462-g59: نه starts the negative comparative structure; گویندگان is the plural subject 'speakers/narrators'. بر درش is a locative phrase 'at his door/gate' (بر + در + ـش 'his'), and کمترند is a plural predicative adjective/verb 'are fewer' (کمتر + اند), agreeing with the plural subject گویندگان.
segments.s462-g60: که here links the two halves in a comparison ('than' / 'that'), introducing the standard of comparison. بر تارک بخردان is a prepositional phrase meaning 'upon the crown/top (of the head) of the wise' (بر + تارک + بخردان), and افسرند is a nominal-predicate with the plural copula -ند attached to افسر (helmet), so the clause reads 'that [there are] helmets on the brows of the wise'. Note: تارک is an archaic noun 'top/crown (of the head)' used here; افسرند is a third-person plural nominal predicate ('are helmets').
segments.s462-g61: کجا is an interrogative adverb 'where/how' introducing a rhetorical question. خود (here) is emphatic 'himself' placed before the verb to stress the subject. پیام is the direct object 'message/tidings', and آرد is the classical present/subjunctive form of آوردن 'to bring' (here 'would bring' / 'bring forth'); از خویشتن is an ablative phrase 'from his own self' (از + خویشتن) specifying origin of the tidings.
segments.s462-g62: چنان qualifies شهریاری: 'such sovereignty/kingly power'. سر انجمن is a genitival/nominal phrase 'head (chief) of the assembly/council' where سر is used metaphorically as 'chief/leader' and انجمن is the body over which that head presides. Together the phrase qualifies the kind of sovereignty mentioned earlier.
segments.s462-g63: سکندر is the subject 'Alexander'. بدان مایه is an adverbial/quantifying phrase 'with/that amount' (بدان = به + آن, 'by/with that'); note the segmentation here treats ب and دان separately. دارد is the present-tense auxiliary/lexical verb 'has/possesses', and خرد is the object 'wisdom'. Overall the clause states possession: 'Alexander has that much wisdom.'
segments.s462-g64: که introduces a result/relative clause 'that/so that' describing what his wisdom enables. از رای پیشینگان is a prepositional phrase 'from the opinion/counsel of the predecessors' (از + رای + پیشینگان). بگذرد is the present/subjunctive form of گذشتن 'to pass' used here in the sense 'to go beyond / to depart from' (i.e., to transcend or disregard previous counsel).
segments.s462-g49: تو گفتی is 2nd-person past (تو + گفتی) 'you said'; که introduces the reported clause. دارا + ست is the classical pairing here (دارا 'Dara' or 'one who has' + ست = 'is'); بر تخت عاج is a prepositional phrase 'on an ivory throne' (preposition بر + object تخت عاج) that follows the copula/verb as is typical in classical Persian word order.
segments.s462-g50: This is a coordinated noun phrase listing attributes/possessions: items are joined by و 'and'; با before فر marks 'with/glory' and links that item to تاج. The phrase functions as accompaniment/ornamentation of the throne (i.e. 'with X and Y and with glory and crown').
segments.s462-g51: بدو is a prepositional pronoun 'to him' (introducing the indirect object); گفت here functions as 'he said' (classical past). نام و نژاد تو چیست is a direct question 'what are your name and ancestry?' with چی(ست) 'what is' placed at the end, the normal position for predicate interrogatives.
segments.s462-g52: که begins a relative/explanatory clause 'who/that'; بر فر و شاخت is a prepositional phrase 'on (the) glory and (the) horn' modifying نشان 'sign/token'. کییست is an archaic/poetic interrogative 'who is it?' — here asking whose emblem/mark is borne on those things.
segments.s462-g53: A prepositional/partitive phrase beginning with از 'beyond/from' + اندازه 'measure/degree' + کهتران 'the lesser ones', followed by برتری 'superiority' — together meaning 'a superiority beyond the ordinary measure of the lesser (men)'. The head noun برتری states the quality being described.
segments.s462-g54: من is the first-person subject 'I'. ایدون is an archaic/poetic verb meaning 'it appears/comes (to me)' or 'I judge', and گمانم 'I think/my belief' reinforces the speaker's opinion; که introduces the content clause 'that he is Iskandar (Alexander)'. The clause expresses the speaker's conjecture.
segments.s462-g55: بدین is a classical prepositional compound meaning 'by/with this' (ب + دین) and introduces a list of accompanying qualities. The nouns فر, بالا, گفتار, چهر are linked by و 'and' forming a series of attributes ('glory and highness and speech and face/countenance').
segments.s462-g56: مگر here introduces a rhetorical question ('is it not that...' / 'did not...'), تخت را is the definite object 'the throne' (را marks the object). پروریدت is a classical past verb 'nurtured/raised' with the enclitic -ت 'you' as the object, and سپهر 'the sky/heaven' appears at the end as the agent — literal reading: 'Did not the sky raise the throne for you?'.
segments.s462-g73: چو is a temporal conjunction 'when'. Here the clause begins with the subject سکندر followed by چو + the (archaic/poetic) past verb خوردی meaning 'drank' (classical past ending -ی used here for the narrative past). می‌خوشگوار is a compound modifier (می ‘wine’ + خوشگوار ‘delightful’) describing what he drank; it functions as the object phrase of خوردی.
segments.s462-g74: نهادی is the past main verb (from نهادن ‘to place/set’) meaning 'he set/placed'. سبک modifies جام and means 'light(ly)' here. جام را contains the object plus the definite object marker را, and بر کنار is an adverbial phrase 'to the side/aside'. Word order is verb + (adverbial) phrase following the verb in this poetic line.
segments.s462-g75: چنین تا 'thus until' introduces a continuing action. می‌و جام here functions as a coordinated/compound subject ('the wine and the cup' or the wine-in-the-cup) and چندی بگشت means 'turned/went round for a while' — چندی is an adverb 'for a while'. بگشت is an older poetic past meaning 'went around/was passed about'.
segments.s462-g76: نهادن is a verbal noun (the act of 'placing/putting'); ز here is the poetic form of از 'from', and اندازه means 'measure/moderation'. اندر گذشت (گذشت 'passed') is a past-clause saying that the putting 'passed beyond measure' — idiomatically, 'his putting it down exceeded moderation' (i.e. went too far).
segments.s462-g77: دهنده is the subject 'the giver/donor'. بیامد is the past 'came'. به + دارا is the prepositional phrase 'to Dārā' (proper name of the king here), and بگفت is the reporting verb 'he said' that introduces the reported clause.
segments.s462-g78: که introduces reported speech ('that...'). رومی شد is 'became/was a Roman' or 'a Roman turned up/appeared' (Rûm/Byzantine). امروز is the temporal adverb 'today'. با جام جفت is a predicative phrase with جفت 'pair/mate' governed by شد — literally 'paired with the cup' or 'matched to the cup' (i.e. someone became the cup's companion).
segments.s462-g79: بفرمود is an honorific/polite reporting verb 'he commanded/said'. تا introduces the subordinate purpose/result clause ('that/so that'). زو is the fused form of ز + او = 'from him' (i.e. 'ask him'); بپرسند is subjunctive/imperative sense 'they should ask' (3rd plural). شاه here is vocative/address 'O king' or marks the addressee in the reported ordering.
segments.s462-g80: که introduces the content of the question. جام نبید = 'the cup of wine' (نبید = wine). از چه is the idiomatic interrogative 'for what reason' = 'why'. داری is present 'you have/hold/keep'. نگاه here participates in the idiom جام را نگاه داشتن 'to keep/retain the cup' (the noun 'نگاه' or verbal sense 'holding in view' is used poetically), so the whole clause asks why the king keeps the wine-cup.
